title: [RFE] login with client inside a container when host socket is mounted
body:
Hi all,
I'm trying to build images with podman-py but can't use base images from a private registry, despite pulling them work after login.
Note: this is done inside a container started with "--userns=keep-id" and -v "/run/user/XXX/podman/podman.sock:/run/podman/podman.sock:z"

```python
import podman
client = podman.PodmanClient(base_url="/run/podman/podman.sock")
client.login(registry='my_private_reg', username=..., password=...)
client.images.pull('my_private_reg/docker/python:3.14')  # Works as expected
# Dockerfile contains "FROM my_private_reg/docker/python:3.12"
client.images.build(dockerfile='Dockerfile', ...)  # Fails
```
Error message:
```
podman.errors.exceptions.BuildError: creating build container: initializing source docker://my_private_reg/docker/python:3.12: reading manifest 3.12 in my_private_reg/docker/python: unauthorized: The client does not have permission for manifest: Download request for repo:path 'docker-cache:python/3.12/list.manifest.json' is forbidden for user: 'anonymous'.
```

comments.body:
hey, thanks for the report

My first guess is that it's not about being in a container but it has to do with `build` and `pull`. I'll take a look

I tried with a private registry and couldn't reproduce. just making sure, did you set `--security-opt label=disable` as per [the docs](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-system-service.1.html#access-the-unix-socket-from-inside-a-container)?

Hi, sorry for the delay. You cannot reproduce the error because you are probably already registered on your system. Please remove any Podman (and Docker) auth file such as `${XDG_RUNTIME_DIR}/containers/auth.json` and `$HOME/.docker/config.json` before proceeding with the test. The error occurs with or without `--security-opt label=disable`

I think I was able to reproduce something similar with the few commands in the first comment.
Now, I believe it's not a bug but rather a missing feature.

Here are the results of my investigation.

---

Preparation:

1. create a container to use for the reproducer
```
FROM python:trixie

RUN pip install podman
```

`podman build . --tag podman-py:trixie`

2. push a private image on a registry (I have `docker.io/inknos/inknos-test-private:tagname`)

3. create a Containerfile in a test directory that will use FROM private image:

```
mkdir test
cat > test/Containerfile << EOF
FROM docker.io/inknos/inknos-test-private:tagname
RUN echo "hello world"
EOF
```

4. Before every step, remove dangling images with `podman image prune -a` and remove logins with `rm $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/containers/auth.json`

5. Run the container with `podman run --rm -it --security-opt label=disable -v /run/user/1000/podman/podman.sock:/run/podman/podman.sock:z -v $PWD:/test:Z podman-py:trixie`. Run these commands to check the sock connection

```python
from podman import PodmanClient                                 
client = PodmanClient(base_url="unix:///run/podman/podman.sock")
client.images.list()                                            
```

Note: if `--security-opt label=disable` is not set, step 5 fails on Fedora.

---

Reproduce:

**Case 1**: login inside the container, no login on host, pull | build

```
>>> client.images.pull("docker.io/inknos/inknos-test-private:tagname")
...
    raise APIError(cause, response=self._response, explanation=message)
podman.errors.exceptions.APIError: 403 Client Error: Forbidden ({"message":"denied: requested access to the resource is denied"}
)

>>> client.images.build(dockerfile="/test/Containerfile", path="/test")
...
podman.errors.exceptions.BuildError: creating build container: unable to copy from source docker://inknos/inknos-test-private:tagname: initializing source docker://inknos/inknos-test-private:tagname: reading manifest tagname in docker.io/inknos/inknos-test-private: requested access to the resource is denied
```

Image not pulled, image not built, as expected

**Case 2**: login inside the container, no login on host, pull | build

```
>>> client.login(registry="docker.io", username="inknos", password="xxx")
{'IdentityToken': '', 'Status': 'Login Succeeded'}
```

```
>>> client.images.pull("docker.io/inknos/inknos-test-private:tagname")
...
    raise APIError(cause, response=self._response, explanation=message)
podman.errors.exceptions.APIError: 403 Client Error: Forbidden ({"message":"denied: requested access to the resource is denied"}

>>> client.images.build(dockerfile="/test/Containerfile", path="/test")
...
    raise BuildError(result["error"], report_stream)
podman.errors.exceptions.BuildError: creating build container: unable to copy from source docker://inknos/inknos-test-private:tagname: initializing source docker://inknos/inknos-test-private:tagname: reading manifest tagname in docker.io/inknos/inknos-test-private: requested access to the resource is denied
```

Image not pulled, image not built. Token is fetched from socket connection

**Case 3**: no login inside the container, login on host, pull | build

```
$ podman login docker.io
Username: inknos
Password: 
Login Succeeded!
```

```
>>> client.images.pull("docker.io/inknos/inknos-test-private:tagname")
<Image: 'docker.io/inknos/inknos-test-private:tagname'>
>>> client.images.build(dockerfile="/test/Containerfile", path="/test")
(<Image: ''>, <itertools._tee object at 0x7f16864cc740>)
```

Note: local image removed after every step, credentials removed and refreshed after every step.

Image pulled, image built. Token is fetched from host

---

The behavior is consistent with pull and build. To be able to login from inside the container while connected to host socket is probably not a bug, I'd say is a feature request.

Please tell me if I missed something from your example, or provide more details :)

After removing any dangling image before the pull, I also have the same result :-p
Now, is there a way with a feature request to be able to only login via PodmanPy without having to do a 'podman login' on the host? It's a problem on CI with Jenkins...
I think it's a very reasonable feature, although I don't know the details of how the token travels through the socket, so I'll need to investigate it
@lnlrbr do you think the title fits? I think the result wanted is more clear now

Ok for the updated title.
BTW, what is the point of doing a `client.login` ?

It seems there is no libpod API to authenticate and store persistent credentials as the Podman CLI does.

Solutions include:
1. wrap podman cli login command around `podman.domain.systemSystemManager.login()`
2. directly read and write to `auth.json` following Podman CLI specs

@inknos what do you think?
